sentences.text: Yeah.
So, we-- we weren't that this is the last week, but we knew that things are coming up very soon.

So a cousin of my, who already, weeks before, tried to organize a-- like a partisan-- and he talked to some young men-- he used to come into-- to the camp, the concentration camp at night, and they used-- he used to talk [? cover ?] with certain-- with men that he knew them.
And-- and to organize that they would escape during the night.
And-- and he made already-- sort of-- he looked around in-- in the forest, a place that we would be able to-- to-- to go there.
He wasn't really in camp.
He-- I don't know what he was doing.
He-- that time, he lived in Lwow.
A young boy, he was only 19 or 20.
He--
His Jewish name, Abraham.
But we called him [NON-ENGLISH].
Very nice, handsome young guy.
And a lot of courage.
I mean, he just-- he really wanted to do anything possible to-- to-- not to let us, you know, just be killed.
So he organized quite a few young men, strong, nice guys.
And then he-- and-- woman, he didn't want-- I mean, he just wanted men that can fight back.
But whoever had a sister, a girlfriend, a wife, or something.
So anyway, I was his cousin.
So he took me that time.
How did he get in touch with you?
He used to come in, like I said, during the night.
And being that we were on this-- on this field, in the last-- the last weeks, we were practically sleeping every night there.
After dinner, we used to-- instead of going to this house, you know, where they-- where they housed the woman, we slept in the field, so he can--
There weren't that time too many guards at night.
This was not-- this was not a camp that was surrounded with-- with wire.
This was sort of like a open field.
So he came that time and he took me out.
And just-- I guess but a week after, they came in and they liquidated the-- the camp.
So I-- I was in the forest with him.
Who liquidated the camp?
The Germans.
Yeah.
[SIGHS] So, a lot of peoples runs.
They-- they-- they escaped.
Whichever way where they were, where they worked, that they could, you know, run away, they-- they run away.
But most of them, they were just taken on-- on the-- on trucks and this-- took to-- to-- to the trains, where-- I don't even know where they took them afterwards.

I-- a lot of the times I go to-- here to the holocaust, you know, that they have here in Mount Sinai, I don't even know which way, because there's so many places where they took-- I don't even know where they took my parents.
So anyway, he took me to the-- and I was there from-- they liquidated, I think, in-- during summer, in July, June, July.
And we spent in there, [SIGHS] in the forest, a whole summer and a whole winter-- I mean, the rest of the summer and the whole winter.
Can you describe how you lived while you were there?
How we lived in the beginning was very, very hard.
Because, see, the-- the forest was-- again, we going back to Hanaczow.
The forest was-- Hanaczow was surrounded with this forest.
And we were very familiar-- and this cousin of my who-- who made this partisan, you know, a group-- he knew, very well, the forest.

And we-- at first, during summer, we had like, like out of a-- like the soldiers have the-- the fabric that they make the-- how you call-- that we can-- you know, when it rains, or something, that we could sleep there and we could, you know-- during summer was OK.
How you call the-- oh, gosh.
Tents.
Right.
He made a tent, a big, big tent.
And there were, I think, about 30 people there.
And with food was a problem.
We had to go down to the-- to the village, to Hanaczow.
And some of the gentiles, you know, they were Polish.
This-- this village was Polish.
So, even some didn't like Jews, but they were also prosecuted from the Ukrainians, because they were-- they hated the Polish people.
So they tried to help us out.
And they tried-- a few of the men, sometimes-- I used to be the guide, you know, to take them-- because I knew the village-- and to buy some food.
Whatever you could-- bread, and this.
And a lot of times, all these-- the young boys, they just went and they-- they-- they just went into some villages, and went in to-- to-- to farmers, and they just took things.
They just-- how would you say, not a holdup-- but, you know, they just, you know, whatever they could find there, they just took.
Because we had nothing to lose and we had nothing to eat.
But mostly, we tried to-- to get from them, to buy.
We still had a little money.
I don't remember how and what, but we still had.
And so we used to buy things and go back.
But then during summer, there were a lot of, you know, like, raspberries, strawberries.
And I remember one time they brought a-- a big sack of-- of sugar.
So I-- I remember, maybe about two, three weeks, we just lived with these berries and sugar.
How I could, you know, consume that much sugar, I'll never know, you know.
A lot of times, I just wonder.
Because this was our main nutrition, main food.
And so this was going on-- you mentioned how-- how we-- and this was going on practically the whole time.
Then-- you know, this was the group that my cousin formed.
But then other groups formed.
And there were quite a few, but a little bit distance, a little-- a few miles away, there was another group.
And a few miles away was another group.
And then also, we were-- quite a few times, we were attacked from the Germans.
Because the-- the Ukrainians let them know that we are hiding there.
They used to surround-- they knew exactly what places and they used to, you know, surround the areas.
And they used to, you know, try to-- they killed-- they killed a lot.
We used to run.
You know, in-- in the forest, there's a lot of bushes and trees and this.
So some could, you know, lie down and-- and-- and-- and-- and hide in all these things, which were very, you know, heavy bushes.
And-- and most of them got killed.
Yeah.
They-- most of them, they-- they did.
But you know, when so many soldiers came and they-- they surrounded, most everybody was running, because there was no way how you can fight.
A few times, they did shoot back and that.
And this happened a few times.
And then also, a few times, I guess the Polish people let us know that they are planning-- sometimes it was-- it was true.
Sometimes it was just, you know, it wasn't so.
But they let us know.
And so we used to leave these places and go to-- you know, just go all night, next day, to much farther places, to-- to-- to leave these, you know, places where we were, you know, housing there.
And a few times, we were lucky because we left.
Sometimes they didn't come.

And also, you know, certain times, we used to go down to the village-- I, and a few more that-- we had these people that we could stay there a couple nights, until-- [SIGHS] until there came a time that they were very angry, the-- the Ukrainians there.
And the Polish.
This was a Polish village.
They were very angry.
And they knew that Jewish people are coming down and they are sort of helping us a little bit out.
So there was-- this-- this village was like a valley and then around, you know, you know, like-- I want to say mountains, but you know, hills.
And they came from the surrounding-- from the Ukrainians' villages.

They came on-- on these, you know, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] you know, so, like, Hanaczow was like a valley, and they try to-- to shoot down, and they-- [SIGHS] and that time, I was, and a few more of us, were also hidden in one of the bunkers there, on the house.
And sudden they started a fire on this-- that they just burned the whole village, from all the sides, you know?
They-- they-- they start, you know, shooting, and coming down and setting out fires to destroy this village.
And then I was, that time, underneath, you know.
The house was burning.
And I don't know.
I, and a few more of us, we sort of managed to-- to-- to-- to come out of this-- of this bunker and go through the-- the-- the house was on fire.
We-- we choked.
And somehow we got out and and we start running, running down the fields, and going back to the-- to the forest.
And [SIGHS] so of course, the Ukrainians came down from these-- what did I say, not-- from these hills.
They-- and they all had rifles.
And they were just, like, you know, they were hunting.
Just like you hunt, you know, animals.
This is what they were doing, running after us.
And most of them got killed.
In fact, next to me, my friends and their mother, there was just a few-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] maybe 20 feet away, they were killed, a mother and two-- two-- two-- daughters.
And I don't know how I escaped.
It was, you know, it was a very difficult time.
They were just running.
They were just hunting, you know?
And then, then we went back to the forest.

But what I'm saying is we-- when-- we-- we were in the forest, but every time something, you know, was supposed to, that we knew that things going to come up, so we used to either change in different places in the forest, or go down to this village, you know, whoever had where to go to hide for a few days.
And this was going on until-- until the end.
Yes.
There was Lola Gruder and-- and Tanya Gruder, [NON-ENGLISH] Gruder.
And this was the mother and sisters of this man who took me out from the-- from the bunker the time to the ghetto that he was a part of, the Judenfrei.
This was the-- his mother and his two sisters.
Yeah.
One was my age.
One was a little bit younger, a couple years younger.
So, and that's-- [SIGHS] that's what I-- I think this was until the end.

And then we were in the forest, and the same thing, you know-- frightened, running, running-- until-- until they came in July-- what was it, in July, when-- when-- when the Russians-- when the Germans started pulling back?
June, July?
Of which year?
Yeah.
And we were up there in the-- in the forest.
There was a lot of forest, a very lot of forestry.
And we were there and we were up-- you know, there were hills.
We were going up on the hills to see what's going on.
Because next to the forest was a lot of fields and there was a road.
And we wanted to see because we could hear, you know, planes and shooting and all.
We knew a war is going on, but we didn't know who is where and what.
So we went up to the-- on these hills and to see what's going on.
So we could see, you know, soldiers and tanks, you know, moving, forward, backward.
But we didn't know if this is German or they are-- they're-- they are Russians.

And then, little by little, we started going down from the forest, which there were a lot of fields with-- it was that time that the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, it was like the-- all the grains were, you know, about to be, like, how you-- the grains were ready to be-- wheat, and-- and rye, and oh, I don't know, corn.
And they were ripe to be cut, you know.
But they were still there and, you know, because they grew sort of high.
So we started going down the-- the-- the forest and going in to these fields and going closer to the road, to see who-- what kind of soldiers are there.
So then we realized they're-- they're-- we could hear Russian talking.
So we got closer to the road.
And when we saw this we thought, oh, god.
This is something.
[CRYING] So anyway, we came down there.
And we didn't know-- we were so confused.
We didn't know what to do.
And they kept on telling us, don't stay here, because here is going on a fight.
Go back.
Go back a few miles.
Because, you know, we are here now.
But we might not be here tomorrow.
Because they were fighting.
[SIGHS]
So we went to this road and and we start begging to these Russian soldiers to-- because there were tanks and-- and trucks going forward.
They were also going backward, to bring some materials, you know.
So some, which were going back, you know, they took us back to-- to another city-- maybe you heard of it-- Zloczow.
So we went there.
And of course, nothing-- it was all bombarded there.
There was-- the city was like-- like, a disaster.
But there was a building that the Germans used to house there.
So they left-- they left all kind of things, you know.
There were dry-- I remember dry vegetables.
They were, like, packaged, very dry.
You have to put them in water for a couple-- a day or so, to-- to-- to make them-- but anyway, we stayed there.
And we found something to eat.
I don't remember.
And in between, I also came down with a very big boil under my arm.
It was so-- an infection, or something.
And I had temperature.
And I was very sad.
I didn't know what's going on with me.
But anyway, we stayed there for a few days.
[SIGHS] And-- and then we were told that the Germans are pulling back.
They already fired the-- the-- the Lemberg.
Lwow is already free and the other cities are free.
So we came back.
And then where do we go back?
There's no place to go.
So we went back to Hanaczow.
There was-- the-- the-- the-- this village was burned down completely.
Just certain parts of the houses were a little bit, you know, left.
And we stayed there because we had no place to go.
And we stayed there for a few days.
And then we realized that the Ukrainians from around-- surrounding this village, they became very-- they-- they were frightened.
They didn't want Jews to be-- to be around anymore.
So they-- they start attacking us.
So we-- it was a long story.
I'm not going to get into it.
But how we left, you know, this-- Hanaczow.
And we started going towards Lwow.
Do tell me, please.
It's-- it's a long-- it's a very-- we were there for a few days.
And there was nothing to eat.
And there was nothing to do.
So we went to the fields and we start cutting down the-- the grain that was ready to-- to be-- what you-- to be harvest.

And we start, you know-- like wheat, and we start-- like-- with-- with pieces of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] lumber, you know, that-- to-- to-- to-- sort of like knocking, that the grain would loosen up, and come out.
And then we dried this grain.
And then, in some of the houses, there were left things that, you know, that-- that-- I don't know if you ever saw this-- that they put grain and they made like flour.
And then we started making flour out of this grain.
And then I don't remember how and what-- how we did it, but there were still ovens in some of these houses that they were burned down.
There were ovens.
So we baked some bread.
But when I think back, I say, where did we take things to make bread?
I mean, besides the flour, you need other-- I mean-- there was water and flour.
We made bread, I and a-- and a cousin of mine who lives in New York.
I remember we made this-- we baked this bread.
And then we started walking to-- to Lwow, to sell the bread, and-- and to have something.
Because we had no-- no-- nothing.
No place, nowhere to go.
No-- nothing to eat, but that-- we baked this bread.
Anyway, we went that time to Koruwice-- there were-- it was the-- the camp.
And this was the main road to-- to Lwow.
Because the village where we lived, it was what, eight?
I think eight miles to-- to Kurowice, which was the main road to Lemberg.
This was like a side village.
And we start walking.
I got blisters.
And I got-- you know, I didn't have to take off my shoes.
Anyway, my feet were completely, you know, sore, and with blisters.
And we came to-- [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah.
We came to the road and then we start stopping some of the-- of the farmers.
They were going, you know, with the wagons and this, to give us a little bit a ride.
We were telling them that we are going to-- we want to go to-- to Lwow.
So they gave us little rides, you know, as far they used to go.
And then we begged somebody else to give us a little ride until we came to Lwow.
And I-- I don't remember.
We sold this bread and then we-- we didn't have where to go to-- to sleep.
But then we-- I mean, we found out that there is a-- a building that they made available for whoever is left and-- and comes in to-- to Lwow, to have someplace to go in, to sleep through a night.
And also, if anybody's looking for somebody that we can, you know, find whoever's left, that we can find each other.
And of course, I have this-- one of my uncles lived in Lemberg, in-- in a very beautiful area, and this.
When I went there, there was nobody there.
I thought maybe one of his daughters, or-- there was nobody there.
He was killed right away, my uncle.
But anyhow, his wife and his daughters, I didn't find them.

So we went, we slept through I think two or three nights in this-- in this in this room, this building that they reserved for the people, for these, you know, remnants, who were left, and then-- to have someplace to come in.
And then-- and then we started going back to Hanaczow.
How long a distance was it from Lwow to Hanaczow?
It was but, maybe, 35 to maybe more of miles.
But there was going in miles.
Miles were-- I mean, kilometers and miles.
But I don't remember how many kilometers to-- to-- to-- to-- but anyway, it was quite a distance.
And there's also-- there was a train from Kurowice to-- to Lwow.
But not from Hanaczow.
But the trains didn't go that time, and this.
So [SIGHS] we-- [SIGHS] we try to get back to Hanaczow.
So we try to walk again, I with my cousin.
And we walked, you know, till it starts getting dark, and then we stopped in another village that we-- we happened to be, the year before, there for awhile.
When they burned down Hanaczow, we went there and we-- we stayed there for awhile.
What village was this?
Bilka.
Bilka, [NON-ENGLISH], something.
And we stopped there.
And this cousin of my had a-- had a-- a family that they-- she stayed with them, gentiles.
So we came in there and she received us very nice and she gave us something to eat and we slept through the night.
And then in the morning, we were ready to-- to proceed to go to Hanaczow.
And while we left her house and-- and we were, you know, getting ready to go to the main road, there were Russian soldiers.
They-- they-- they were cruising around with big trucks.
And they used to-- whoever they saw on the-- on the-- you know, on the-- the street, they used to pick him up and take him to work.
So they picked us up.
And they took him to another little village, to-- to [NON-ENGLISH].
And we had to stay there all day and work.
So what did we do?
They, you know, they-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] know where the trains go by, you know, the-- how you call the iron things that the train-- The rails?
Yeah.
The rails were all, you know, from-- from fighting and bombarding and this, these rails were all, you know, broken and this.
So we had to, all day, you know, pick up pieces of-- of iron, with these big screws and take them there to the-- to the rail.
And-- and this was going on all-- all day.
Then they took him-- took us back to-- to the village, to Bilka.
And then next day, we starts running, proceeding to Hanaczow.
And we came almost close, that I felt that my-- my feet were just not, you know, was [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] like fluid from the blisters coming out.
I couldn't walk anymore.
And I said thank god we are here.
And there was some grass.
Instead of gravel, there was grass.
And while we were proceeding this-- Hanaczow, this village, the people who were-- the-- the Jewish people who were still there, we-- we see they're coming toward us.
And they said, don't go there.
We are running away because the Ukrainians are after us.
And they're shooting from all these, you know, surrounding hills.
(SIGHS)
So we turned around and we went back.
So it was-- it was a-- a tough, tough thing.
Then we went-- finally, we went back to-- to Lwow.
And then we-- we stayed there again for a few days.
And then-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] then, of course, I had this friend-- I mean, now, he is my husband.
But that time, he was with me.
And we had no place to go.
And he said to me, you're not going to stay here.
But he was from a different city that-- there was already Polish.
Here was Russia.
And he was, like, from [NON-ENGLISH].
So this was already past [NON-ENGLISH].
And he said, I have to go there and I have to see if somebody is still alive.
And so he, you know--
--hijacked-- Yeah.
OK.
Yeah.
Sure.
